title: The KPZ Universality Class and Equation
speaker: Ivan Corwin
affiliation: Courant Institute of Mathematics, New York University

proof_stages.label: Continuum polymer, SHE, and Hopf-Cole KPZ
proof_stages.summary: The continuum directed random polymer free energy is defined through Brownian bridges in space-time white noise and made rigorous via Wiener chaos, the stochastic heat equation with delta initial data, the Hopf-Cole solution of KPZ, and spatial-noise renormalization.

proof_stages.label: Weak-noise discrete-polymer universality
proof_stages.summary: A discrete directed polymer partition function is shown to be a discrete stochastic heat equation, and under diffusive scaling with beta proportional to epsilon it converges to the continuum SHE/CDRP object.

proof_stages.label: Fredholm crossover distribution and scaling corollaries
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem gives an exact contour-integral Fredholm determinant formula for the one-point CDRP/KPZ free-energy distribution, with large-time Tracy-Widom GUE and short-time Gaussian/Edwards-Wilkinson limits.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to weakly asymmetric corner growth/ASEP
proof_stages.summary: For the proof, the corner growth model/asymmetric simple exclusion process is introduced, and a microscopic Hopf-Cole transform under weak asymmetry gamma proportional to epsilon is proved to converge to the stochastic heat equation with delta data.

proof_stages.label: Tracy-Widom exact ASEP formula
proof_stages.summary: The argument imports Tracy and Widom's exact formula for the distribution of a tagged ASEP particle, translated into the height-function variables of the weakly asymmetric growth model.

proof_stages.label: Critical asymptotics to the KPZ Fredholm kernel
proof_stages.summary: The weak-asymmetry/KPZ scaling is inserted into the exact ASEP formula and a delicate steepest-descent analysis with nearly colliding contours yields the Fredholm determinant formula for the crossover law.

proof_stages.label: KPZ fixed point and further distributional problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the one-point formula within broader unresolved questions about the KPZ renormalization fixed point, other initial data, multipoint and multitime distributions, and the need for more robust continuum solvability.

examples.name: Last-passage percolation as the beta-infinity polymer
examples.note: The beta-infinity limit of the discrete polymer is used to make the strong-noise/KPZ-fixed-point question concrete and to recall the Airy_2/Tracy-Widom behavior expected in the large-time limit.
examples.engagement_reason: He defines last-passage percolation as the beta-to-infinity limit of the polymer free energy, writes the max-over-paths last-passage time, and discusses its KPZ scaling limit.

passes.reason: Last-passage percolation is the zero-temperature/infinite-beta polymer, replacing the partition sum by a max over paths.
It is the zero-temperature beta=infinity limit where the polymer sum collapses to maximizing paths.
As the β=∞ zero-temperature limit, last-passage percolation collapses the polymer free energy to a maximum over paths and is used as a concrete model, not a refutation.
as the beta-infinity/zero-temperature limit it collapses the polymer partition sum to the maximum passage time over paths
β=∞ is the zero-temperature max-plus limit of the directed polymer, collapsing the Gibbs measure onto longest paths and used as a concrete model rather than a disproof.
The beta-infinity directed polymer is the zero-temperature/max-plus limit where the partition sum collapses to the maximizing path, an extreme boundary case rather than a refutation.
Last-passage percolation is the zero-temperature β=∞ polymer where the partition sum collapses to a maximum over paths.
Last-passage percolation is the zero-temperature/β=∞ polymer limit, where the partition sum collapses to the maximizing path, and it is used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
β=∞ is the zero-temperature boundary limit where the polymer partition sum collapses to a maximizing last-passage path.
It is used as the zero-temperature β=∞ endpoint where the polymer partition function becomes a maximum over paths.
It is the zero-temperature beta-infinity limit where the polymer partition sum collapses to the maximum-weight path, used as a concrete KPZ model.
used as the zero-temperature beta equals infinity limit selecting the maximal-weight path, not to refute a claim

examples.name: Superdiffusive polymer-path wandering
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes polymer paths in positive-temperature polymer or last-passage models wandering transversely on the t^{2/3} scale, illustrating the KPZ exponent relations.
examples.engagement_reason: He states qualitatively that polymer paths wander on the t^{2/3} transverse scale and mentions the KPZ exponent relation, but does not derive or check it.

passes.reason: The t^{2/3} transversal wandering is a typical KPZ-polymer scaling phenomenon, not a sporadic, collapsed, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
The t^{2/3} wandering is presented as the typical KPZ polymer fluctuation behavior, not as a sporadic or limiting object.
The t^{2/3} superdiffusive wandering is presented as standard KPZ-polymer fluctuation behavior rather than as a structurally special object or counterexample.
the t^{2/3} transversal wandering is presented as the characteristic KPZ polymer behavior rather than a special object or failure case
The t^{2/3} transverse wandering is the standard KPZ superdiffusive path behavior being described, not a sporadic or adversarial object.
The t^{2/3} transverse wandering is described as characteristic KPZ polymer behavior, not a sporadic, symmetric, or optimal object and not used to disprove a claim.
The t^{2/3} path wandering is presented as the typical KPZ polymer fluctuation behavior, not as an obstruction.
The t^{2/3} transverse wandering is presented as the usual KPZ polymer-path behavior, not as a sporadic, limiting, maximally symmetric, or adversarial object.
superdiffusive t^{2/3} wandering is presented as the expected KPZ-class behavior of polymer paths, not as a refutation.
It describes the characteristic KPZ transverse wandering of typical polymer paths, not a failure case or special object.

examples.name: Line-to-point and half-line white-noise initial data
examples.note: Alternative polymer geometries and a half-line white-noise potential are cited as illustrative extensions of one-point solvability beyond delta/point-to-point initial data, including a crossover to the BBP transition.
examples.engagement_reason: He lists line-to-point and half-line white-noise initial data as further solvable or interesting cases and states known distributional connections without working through them.

passes.reason: The half-line white-noise data with minus infinity on the other half is a singular one-sided limiting initial condition, while it is used as another solvable case rather than to refute anything.
The half-line white-noise data with -infinity on the other half-line is a boundary-type initial condition used as another solvable geometry.
The half-line white-noise initial condition with −∞ on the other half is a hard boundary/limiting initial datum used as another solvable case, not to disprove anything.
the half-line white-noise setup is a boundary initial condition with the other half sent to minus infinity, used as a solvable extension rather than an obstruction
The line/half-line initial-data variants include a one-sided hard-boundary/limiting case used to extend solvable distributions, not to refute a claim.
The line/half-line initial data are boundary initial-data geometries, especially the one-sided white-noise case at an edge crossover, introduced as further solvable cases rather than counterexamples.
The half-line white-noise data with −∞ on the other half is a singular restricted initial condition used as another solvable case.

These are special boundary/crossover initial-data geometries, especially the half-line white-noise with a -∞ cutoff at the edge of a rarefaction fan, used as solvable extensions rather than counterexamples.

These are introduced as alternate KPZ initial geometries, with the half-line case a boundary/crossover regime.

examples.name: De Moivre central-limit theorem analogy
examples.note: The simple symmetric random walk leading to the Gaussian limit is used as an analogy for deriving information about a continuum stochastic object from an exactly solvable discrete model.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses De Moivre’s central-limit theorem proof as an analogy to solvability via a simple random walk, but only summarizes the idea rather than performing the calculation.

passes.reason: The analogy uses the simple symmetric random walk/binomial model, a highly regular symmetric solvable model for deriving the Gaussian CLT.
